summary: I was born in Brooklyn, New York, in the mid-1950s. Brooklyn is the largest borough both geographically and population-wise, and it was a vibrant melting pot of cultures and languages. Growing up there, I was surrounded by neighbors and friends from Italian and Irish backgrounds, which shaped my early experiences and worldview. The diverse smells and languages of Brooklyn definitely influenced how I saw the world.

My father was a professor of physics at Cooper Union, a small private school in New York City. Cooper Union was founded by Peter Cooper to provide education based on ability rather than wealth, which allowed many working-class kids to attend. This philosophy deeply influenced my family and me, as it emphasized merit and opportunity. My dad's role there connected me to a legacy of accessible, high-quality education.

Peter Cooper was the founder of Cooper Union, where my father taught physics. He established the school with the idea that anyone, regardless of wealth, could get a good education if they had the ability. This vision shaped the educational environment that my family was part of and influenced my upbringing. Knowing about Peter Cooper's commitment to accessible education gave me a strong sense of social justice and empowerment.

Jody Stecher is my older brother, six years my senior, and a renowned musician. He started playing music at a very young age, beginning with an old Martin guitar that belonged to our mom. By age 16, he was playing mandolin with the Greenbrier Boys, a popular folk group in the 1960s. Jody's musical talent and career were a significant part of our family's life and influenced my own relationship with music.

The Martin guitar was the first instrument my brother Jody played seriously. It originally belonged to our mother and was his starting point before he quickly surpassed his teacher. That guitar was a key part of his early musical development, leading him to become a skilled mandolin player with the Greenbrier Boys. The Martin guitar symbolizes the beginning of a lifelong musical journey in our family.

The Greenbrier Boys were a folk group that my brother Jody played with when he was just 16. In the 1960s, they were one of the most popular folk bands on the radio across the country, especially known for their authentic sound. Jody won mandolin contests and was invited to sub with them, marking an important milestone in his musical career. Their music was a big part of our family's connection to folk traditions.

My mother and her sister Greta were avid fans of folk music and collected every issue of Sing Out! magazine and its predecessors. These publications were central to the people's music movement for justice and were part of our household library. Through Sing Out!, we connected with the broader folk music community and the social movements tied to it, which influenced our family culture deeply.

I attended a labor arts exchange at the George Meany Center in Washington, D.C., when I was about 45 years old. This center was a hub for celebrating labor culture and later became the National Labor College. It was there that I had a memorable conversation with Pete Seeger, who mentioned my Aunt Greta's involvement with the Weavers. The center represents an important place where labor and music intersected in my life.

The National Labor College evolved from the George Meany Center, where I participated in labor arts events. This institution celebrates labor culture and education, connecting music and activism. My experiences there, including meeting folk legends like Pete Seeger, deepened my appreciation for the role of music in social movements. It was a meaningful place in my adult life related to labor and cultural history.

Pete Seeger was a legendary folk musician who I met at a labor arts exchange at the George Meany Center. He surprised me by telling me that my Aunt Greta had been a member of the Weavers, the famous folk group he was part of. Pete's presence and stories connected me personally to the folk music movement and its history. His influence and the conversations we shared were significant moments in my understanding of music and activism.

The Weavers were a renowned folk group that included my Aunt Greta as one of the three women members alongside Jackie Alper and Ronnie Gilbert. Though Greta and Jackie did not continue as performers, they remained committed to the movement. The Weavers were known for their practiced, accomplished performances, and my aunt's involvement connected our family to this important chapter in folk music history. Their music was intertwined with social justice causes that mattered to us.

Jackie Alper was one of the three women in the Weavers along with my Aunt Greta and Ronnie Gilbert. Although she did not continue performing extensively with the group, she stayed active in the movement. Jackie and Greta shared a close bond through their music and activism. Their participation in the Weavers was a significant part of the folk music legacy that influenced my family.

Ronnie Gilbert was a member of the Weavers and a longtime friend of my Aunt Greta. Ronnie eventually left the United States for Canada before returning to start her solo career and work with Holly Near. Although my aunt passed away before Ronnie's later career, their friendship was an important connection in our family's folk music history. Ronnie's story is part of the larger narrative of folk music and activism that shaped my upbringing.

Holly Near was a folk singer who collaborated with Ronnie Gilbert after Ronnie returned from Canada. Though my Aunt Greta had already passed away by then, Holly Near was part of the folk music scene connected to our family through Ronnie. Holly's work represents the continuation of the social justice and musical traditions that were important to us. Her career is part of the broader folk movement that influenced my family's values.

Milton Stecher was my father, a physics professor and a man with a deep Jewish cultural background. Although he didn't identify with religion, he brought the Jewish tradition of rigorous argument and analysis into our home. He taught me early math concepts through playful exercises like drawing letters on his back. His survivalist mindset, shaped by the Great Depression, meant he was skilled at making and fixing things, and he valued independence from market dependence.

Judaism was part of my father's cultural heritage, though he did not practice religion himself. He embraced the Jewish style of learning, which involves arguing and debating to arrive at truth, and this intellectual wrestling was a big part of our household. This tradition influenced how I think and engage with ideas, fostering a love for analysis and discussion. The cultural practices around studying the Torah shaped our family's approach to knowledge and reasoning.

The Torah is central to the Jewish tradition of study that my father valued, even though he was not religious. The method of learning involves taking premises from the Torah and arguing them out to deepen understanding. This approach to the Torah influenced how my family engaged with ideas—through debate and intellectual rigor. It was less about faith and more about sharpening analytical skills, which shaped my upbringing.

The Great Depression had a profound impact on my father, Milton Stecher, who was a child during that time. It shaped his survivalist attitude and his belief in self-reliance rather than dependence on the market. He learned many practical skills like baking, upholstering, and fixing cars, which he passed on through example. This experience influenced the values of independence and resourcefulness that I grew up with.
